EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second, very strong, winter storm impacted Southwestern California. Rainfall totals were very significant across the area. Los Angeles county reported rain totals of 4 to 14 inches, Ventura county reported 2 to 10 inches, Santa Barbara county reported 4 to 12 inches and San Luis Obispo county reported 2 to 5 inches. With the heavy rainfall, numerous reports of flooding were received. In the mountains, very significant snowfall was reported with amounts in the 1 to 3 foot range. Also, severe thunderstorms and tornadoes were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automated sensor at Sierra Pelona West reported thunderstorm wind gusts of 75 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-moving cold front came through California on January 31, bringing widespread rain and high winds. A narrow cold frontal rainband (NCFR) developed during the afternoon and evening commute, with moderate to heavy rain across the region as a result. Impacts and post-frontal showers continued through February 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a house, vehicle, and power lines in Saratoga, trapping one person inside the vehicle, who was taken to a hospital with non-life threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stronger and colder more classic winter storm followed Wednesday through|early Thursday, bringing additional wind and snow impacts to the Sierra and western NV. This storm system���s trajectory allowed it to drop southward into eastern California and western Nevada Wednesday-Wednesday night. This storm started with a short round of strong winds followed by bursts of heavier snow. Although this storm appeared to have a more overland trajectory with liquid precipitation totals a little lower, the forcing from this low and attendant cold front remained sufficiently intact to produce additional snow with at least an even chance of 6 or more inches in the Tahoe basin southward to western Mono County, and an additional foot or more along the Sierra crest. Plenty of winter travel hazards at all elevations continued into the Thursday AM commute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (XMPC1-Mean Peak) located 6.0 mi NNE of Leavitt Meadows, CA, and mesonet station (MTH01-MAMMOTH) located 0.9 mi S of Mammoth Mountain, CA,  reported a peak wind gusts of 83 mph, and 129 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust upper trough that dug across the interior West Saturday and Sunday, |aligned with the left exit region of a meridional jet favoring large scale ascent across the Sierra and western Nevada. This setup for strong winds and widespread precipitation across the region. This included the potential for heavy Sierra snowfall with light snow accumulation on valley floors. As the first snow arrived Saturday morning wind observations over Sierra ridges showed gusts ranging 70-80 mph. This system was considered the coldest and most impactful storm up to this point this season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH01-MAMMOTH) located 0.9 mi S of Mammoth Mountain, CA,  reported a peak wind gust 139 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm was allowed to tap into Pacific moisture for a longer period of time that nudged it just enough north to make a more direct landfall into the central OR coast.  This next storm Friday evening into Saturday brought more gusty winds, Sierra snow, and valley rain/snow showers. This storm���s warmer nature raised snow levels a bit higher(5500-6500 feet) with the potential for mixed precipitation in the valley areas and foothills. The lower-end ensemble snow scenarios showed a bit more confident projection for at least a foot of snow in the Sierra with potential for 18-24 inches along the Sierra crest. Regardless, this contributed to another bout of travel disruptions, especially for ski commutes during the upcoming holiday weekend. Wind speeds have trended up a bit with this storm as well with the potential for wind gusts 40 to 50 mph for the valley areas. Sierra ridge wind gusts got a boost with potential for gusts 90+ mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (DYLC1-Doyle) located 3.0 mi N of Doyle, CA,  reported a peak wind gust 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warmer storm brought more rain, high elevation snow, and gusty southwesterly winds late Tuesday into Wednesday. An upper-level low moving into the Pacific Northwest flattened a persistent ridge on Tuesday afternoon, and allowed warmer subtropical moisture to be transported into our region with the increased more zonally directed westerly flow aloft. Higher ridge level winds increased late Tuesday morning as an upper jet oriented over the region bringing peak overnight wind gusts to around 80-100 mph. Ridge winds remained elevated through Wednesday, with breezy SW winds mixing down to valleys by Wednesday afternoon. The main precipitation band moved into the region by Tuesday PM wrapping up by late Wednesday AM. Snow levels remained a bit higher than previous storms due to the subtropical tap. Snow levels will hovered between 8000-8500 feet during the main precipitation event, briefly topping upwards to 9000 feet at times. Snow accumulations were confined to the high Sierra, with predicted storm totals ranging 2-6 inches above 8000 feet. In terms of liquid totals, the highest values expected stayed along the Sierra crest, generally between 0.75-1 inches. A sharp gradient in liquid precipitation persisted east of the crest due to stronger rain shadowing with 0.25-0.5 inches generally west of US-395 and trace-0.1 inch east of Highway 395.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm was allowed to tap into Pacific moisture for a longer period of time that nudged it just enough north to make a more direct landfall into the central OR coast.  This next storm Friday evening into Saturday brought more gusty winds, Sierra snow, and valley rain/snow showers. This storm���s warmer nature raised snow levels a bit higher(5500-6500 feet) with the potential for mixed precipitation in the valley areas and foothills. The lower-end ensemble snow scenarios showed a bit more confident projection for at least a foot of snow in the Sierra with potential for 18-24 inches along the Sierra crest. Regardless, this contributed to another bout of travel disruptions, especially for ski commutes during the upcoming holiday weekend. Wind speeds have trended up a bit with this storm as well with the potential for wind gusts 40 to 50 mph for the valley areas. Sierra ridge wind gusts got a boost with potential for gusts 90+ mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active weather returns Wednesday afternoon to close out the month of January. Therefore, strong and gusty winds especially over higher mountain elevations was followed with mountain snow and valley rain showers. Wind was the first hazard expected starting over the Sierra and NE CA, then spreading into western Nevada with south winds between 30-50 mph, and gusts over 60-80 mph across higher mountain ridges and passes. Valley winds were projected to be 15-35 mph with gusts up to 55 mph. The probability of 60 mph winds across mountain areas in CA was 60% or greater and 10-20% over W NV. Lake winds were around 15-25 mph with gusts to 35-45 mph.||Valley rain and mountain snow showers were projected to move into the region   Wednesday PM. Global models and their respective ensemble members initially dug the trough and a deeper layer of sub-tropical IVT moisture will quickly sweep east and south across portions of northeast California, before descending into the Sierra, western Nevada, and Mono Co. Snow levels started near 7000 feet on Wednesday, then dropped to 6000 feet by Thursday. Most higher elevation areas received mostly rain initially before transitioning to mostly snow Thursday evening. Snow levels dropped to 5500 ft on Thursday afternoon, and dropped further to below 4000 feet by Friday morning. The Sierra above 7000 and Mono Co were given even chances for about 1-2 feet of new snow with 6-12 inches below 7000 feet. For northeast California 4-10 inches of accumulated snow, and up to 20 inches in the higher mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH22-MAMMOTH) located 1.0 mi ESE of Mammoth Mountain, CA reported a peak wind gust of 99 mph.

The following are the strongest winds recorded across the zone:||Rialto (SCE701): 60 MPH|Highland (383SCE): 57 MPH|North Wildwood Park (SCE768): 57 MPH|CSU San Bernardino (SCE809): 57 MPH|Rialto (SCE846): 57 MPH.||Highway 74 was closed in both directions between Theda Street and Spring Street due to a overturned big rig during the mid-morning hours on January 11.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska on February 29 and had deepened off the Pacific Northwest coast on March 1 spreading widespread precipitation as far south a central California. The trough then slowly moved east across the Pacific Northwest on March 2 and 3 before moving east of the region on March 4. This system brought moderate to heavy precipitation to the Sierra Nevada which picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation north of Kings Canyon and 1 to 2 inches further south. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet for the duration of the event and produced 2 to 4 feet on new snowfall north of Kings Canyon and 1.5 to 3 feet of snow further south. This system also produced a half inch to an inch of rainfall in Merced County and a quarter to half inch across most of the remainder of the San Joaquin Valley. Most of the Kern County Mountains and Deserts picked up 1 to 2 tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong winds also accompanied this system and combined with the snow to produce near blizzard conditions over the Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon between the morning of March 1 and the morning of March 3. Scattered thunderstorms also broke out during the afternoons of March 1 and March 2 over parts of the San Joaquin Valley. While the thunderstorms were not heavy rain producers, several funnel clouds were observed and one cell produced a weak tornado in northeast Madera during the afternoon of March 1. In addition to the precipitation, several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 45 mph between the evening of March 2 and the early morning of March 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jawbone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 77 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper low pressure system pushed into the Pacific Northwest on January 6 then dropped southeast into the Great Basin by the morning of January 7, A cold front that accompanied this system crossed central California during the evening of January 6 bringing a period of light to moderate precipitation and strong winds to the area. Several stations in the mountains and deserts of Kern County measured gusts exceeding 45 mph with a few stations exceeding 70 mph. Precipitation amounts were generally a quarter to a half inch from Fresno County northward and a tenth to a quarter inch further south. Elevations above 6000 feet generally picked up between 5  to 10 inches of new snowfall with a few locations in the south end of the Sierra Nevada picking up 12 inches or more. The snow level lowered to 2500 feet before the precipitation ended during the morning of January 7 with several locations in the Sierra foothills and Tehachapi Mountains picking up between 1 and 3 inches of new snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Tehachapi East Valley measured a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper trough moved across central California during the evening of January 10 and the early morning of January 11. This system brought another half inch to an inch of liquid precipitation to the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills north of Kings Canyon with many locations picking up 5 to 10 inches of new snow with a few SNOTELs estimating a foot of new snowfall. Areas further south in the Sierra Nevada and much of the Tehachapi Mountains picked up between a quarter and a half inch while much of the San Joaquin Valley received between a tenth and a quarter inch of rainfall. This system also produced a period of strong wind gusts with several stations in the Tehachapi area and the Mojave Deserts slopes measuring gusts exceeding 50 mph and a few low impact indicator sites reporting gusts exceeding 75 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a tree down on SR 99 south of Ave 48.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that moved across northern California during the evening of January 16 and the early morning of January 17 brought some light precipitation to the area with much of the Sierra Nevada picking up between a quarter and a half inch of liquid precipitation with the snow level near 7000 feet. Several SNOTELs picked up an estimated 3 to 6 inches of new snowfall. Most of the central San Joaquin Valley picked up between a tenth and a quarter inch of rain while the south valley and the Kern County Mountains and Deserts generally had a tenth of an inch or less. This system also produced a brief period of strong wind gusts in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 40 mph and a few locations measuring gusts exceeding 55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bear Peak RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska on February 29 and had deepened off the Pacific Northwest coast on March 1 spreading widespread precipitation as far south a central California. The trough then slowly moved east across the Pacific Northwest on March 2 and 3 before moving east of the region on March 4. This system brought moderate to heavy precipitation to the Sierra Nevada which picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation north of Kings Canyon and 1 to 2 inches further south. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet for the duration of the event and produced 2 to 4 feet on new snowfall north of Kings Canyon and 1.5 to 3 feet of snow further south. This system also produced a half inch to an inch of rainfall in Merced County and a quarter to half inch across most of the remainder of the San Joaquin Valley. Most of the Kern County Mountains and Deserts picked up 1 to 2 tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong winds also accompanied this system and combined with the snow to produce near blizzard conditions over the Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon between the morning of March 1 and the morning of March 3. Scattered thunderstorms also broke out during the afternoons of March 1 and March 2 over parts of the San Joaquin Valley. While the thunderstorms were not heavy rain producers, several funnel clouds were observed and one cell produced a weak tornado in northeast Madera during the afternoon of March 1. In addition to the precipitation, several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 45 mph between the evening of March 2 and the early morning of March 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The co-op station 4SSW Ridgecrest measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This comparatively weaker winter storm opened up another active weather pattern that tracked through the Sierra and W.Nevada late in the evening February 17-18. This storm brought higher elevation snowfall that was heavy at times above 7000 feet. This storm also included the possibility of an additional several feet of snowfall.  Mainly spillover rainfall fell over lower western Nevada valleys that included periods of gusty winds as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH03-MAMMOTH) located 1.7 mi S of Mammoth Mountain, CA reported a peak wind gust of 112 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Additional snow chances continued mid-week, mostly for the northern Sierra. Conditions continued to be treacherous over the Sierra overnight on the 4th with breezy winds, snow, and blowing snow. Conditions briefly improved over the Sierra the morning through the afternoon of the 5th with additional snow shower chances continuing thereafter, especially for the Sierra north of US-50.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH15-Chair 9 Tower 11) located 1.0 mi E of Mammoth Mountain Ski resort reported a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system approached central California on January 31. Gusty southerly winds ahead of this system resulted in some tress being downed and several reports of gusts exceeding 40 mph. Many wind prone locations in the higher elevations measured gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a large tree down blocking both lanes of SR 184 at the Panama Rd. crossing.

CZ_NAME: LOS ANGELES COUNTY SAN GABRIEL VALLEY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late-season, storm brought significant rainfall to the area along with strong thunderstorms. Rainfall totals across the area ranged from 0.50 to 1.50 inches across San Luis Obispo and Santa Barbara counties to 1 to 2 inches across Ventura and Los Angeles counties. Unstable air, associated with the storm, brought scattered strong thunderstorms to the area, producing gusty winds, large hail and even some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather spotter in the Monrovia area reported thunderstorm winds with estimated gusts of 50 MPH. The winds knocked down numerous tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick-hitting storm system produced strong winds over the mountains and desert slopes as well as mountain snow on January 7. Chain controls were in effect across the San Bernardino County Mountains through the late morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds impacted the San Gorgonio Pass near Banning from the evening of January 6 through the morning on January 7. Widespread wind gusts over 35 mph were reported in this area, with a number of gusts over 50 knots. The following are the strongest wind reports in this zone:||Whitewater (SCE1015): 63 mph|Whitewater RAWS: 63 mph|Whitewater Cyn (SCE581): 61 mph|Snowcreek Cyn (SCE577): 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving storm system produced strong winds across much of Southern California January 10-11, with the highest winds recorded in the mountains and along the desert slopes the morning of January 11. Scattered showers occurred mainly across San Diego County, and several inches of snow were recorded in the San Bernardino and Riverside County Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds impacted the San Bernardino County Mountains from the afternoon of January 10 through the late morning hours on January 11. The strongest winds occurred from early to mid-morning on January 11. The following are the strongest winds recorded across the zone:||Burns Canyon: 70 MPH (6284')|Marshall Peak (SDHGPA): 68 MPH (3973')|Big Bear Airport: 62 MPH (6760')|Running Springs: 59 MPH (5445').||A downed tree blocked a lane at Pleasant Drive and Rim of the World SR-18.

This system brought another half inch to an inch of liquid precipitation to the Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills north of Kings Canyon with many locations picking up 5 to 10 inches of new snow with a few SNOTELs estimating a foot of new snowfall. Areas further south in the Sierra Nevada and much of the Tehachapi Mountains picked up between a quarter and a half inch while much of the San Joaquin Valley received between a tenth and a quarter inch of rainfall. This system also produced a period of strong wind gusts with several stations in the Tehachapi area and the Mojave Deserts slopes measuring gusts exceeding 50 mph and a few low impact indicator sites reporting gusts exceeding 75 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tehachapi Airport AWOS measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that moved across northern California during the evening of January 16 and the early morning of January 17 brought some light precipitation to the area with much of the Sierra Nevada picking up between a quarter and a half inch of liquid precipitation with the snow level near 7000 feet. Several SNOTELs picked up an estimated 3 to 6 inches of new snowfall. Most of the central San Joaquin Valley picked up between a tenth and a quarter inch of rain while the south valley and the Kern County Mountains and Deserts generally had a tenth of an inch or less. This system also produced a brief period of strong wind gusts in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 40 mph and a few locations measuring gusts exceeding 55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Paradise Cove measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska on February 29 and had deepened off the Pacific Northwest coast on March 1 spreading widespread precipitation as far south a central California. The trough then slowly moved east across the Pacific Northwest on March 2 and 3 before moving east of the region on March 4. This system brought moderate to heavy precipitation to the Sierra Nevada which picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation north of Kings Canyon and 1 to 2 inches further south. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet for the duration of the event and produced 2 to 4 feet on new snowfall north of Kings Canyon and 1.5 to 3 feet of snow further south. This system also produced a half inch to an inch of rainfall in Merced County and a quarter to half inch across most of the remainder of the San Joaquin Valley. Most of the Kern County Mountains and Deserts picked up 1 to 2 tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong winds also accompanied this system and combined with the snow to produce near blizzard conditions over the Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon between the morning of March 1 and the morning of March 3. Scattered thunderstorms also broke out during the afternoons of March 1 and March 2 over parts of the San Joaquin Valley. While the thunderstorms were not heavy rain producers, several funnel clouds were observed and one cell produced a weak tornado in northeast Madera during the afternoon of March 1. In addition to the precipitation, several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 45 mph between the evening of March 2 and the early morning of March 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station on Grapevine Canyon Rd. measured a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of tropical moisture pushed into central California during the late evening of February 3 and spread inland during the morning of February 4 ahead of a strong low pressure center off the Southern California coast. By the time the precipitation had ended by the evening of February 5, much of the Sierra Nevada above 6000 feet had picked up between 1.5 and 3 feet of new snowfall. Moderate to locally heavy rainfall occurred in the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Sierra as well as across the Kern County Mountains and Deserts where there were several reports of 0.75 to 1.5 inches of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong downslope winds caused considerable damage during on February 4 with several stations reporting gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Pampa Peak measured a peak wind gust of76 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough dropped out of the Gulf of Alaska on February 29 and had deepened off the Pacific Northwest coast on March 1 spreading widespread precipitation as far south a central California. The trough then slowly moved east across the Pacific Northwest on March 2 and 3 before moving east of the region on March 4. This system brought moderate to heavy precipitation to the Sierra Nevada which picked up between 2 and 4 inches of liquid precipitation north of Kings Canyon and 1 to 2 inches further south. The precipitation fell as snow above 5000 feet for the duration of the event and produced 2 to 4 feet on new snowfall north of Kings Canyon and 1.5 to 3 feet of snow further south. This system also produced a half inch to an inch of rainfall in Merced County and a quarter to half inch across most of the remainder of the San Joaquin Valley. Most of the Kern County Mountains and Deserts picked up 1 to 2 tenths of an inch of rainfall. In addition to the precipitation, strong winds also accompanied this system and combined with the snow to produce near blizzard conditions over the Sierra Nevada north of Kings Canyon between the morning of March 1 and the morning of March 3. Scattered thunderstorms also broke out during the afternoons of March 1 and March 2 over parts of the San Joaquin Valley. While the thunderstorms were not heavy rain producers, several funnel clouds were observed and one cell produced a weak tornado in northeast Madera during the afternoon of March 1. In addition to the precipitation, several stations in the Kern County Mountains and Deserts measured wind gusts exceeding 45 mph between the evening of March 2 and the early morning of March 4.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The China Lake NWTC ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong low pressure system which approached the area on January 31 pulled up a deep surge of tropical moisture which brought moderate to heavy precipitation to much of the area on February 1 through the morning of February 2. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains measured between 0.75 inches and 1.50 inches of rainfall while the Sierra Foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rainfall ands the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation fell as snow above 6000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation SNOTELs picking up an estimated 1 to 2 feet of new snow. In addition to the precipitation, there were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 60 mph near Tehachapi and in the Grapevine area during the early morning of February 1. When the main cold front from this system moved across central California during the afternoon of February 1, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills. Some of the storms produced small hail and there were several reports of roadway flooding in Bakersfield. The precipitation tapered off by the morning of February 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison Station at Grapevine Rd. East measured a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong low pressure system which approached the area on January 31 pulled up a deep surge of tropical moisture which brought moderate to heavy precipitation to much of the area on February 1 through the morning of February 2. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Tehachapi Mountains measured between 0.75 inches and 1.50 inches of rainfall while the Sierra Foothills picked up 1 to 2 inches of rainfall ands the higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up 1.5 to 3 inches of liquid precipitation. The precipitation fell as snow above 6000 feet for much of this event with several higher elevation SNOTELs picking up an estimated 1 to 2 feet of new snow. In addition to the precipitation, there were several reports of wind gusts exceeding 60 mph near Tehachapi and in the Grapevine area during the early morning of February 1. When the main cold front from this system moved across central California during the afternoon of February 1, scattered thunderstorms broke out over the San Joaquin Valley and Sierra foothills. Some of the storms produced small hail and there were several reports of roadway flooding in Bakersfield. The precipitation tapered off by the morning of February 2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Cummings Valley measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large slow moving upper low pressure system off the northern California Coast pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin which pushed into central California during the late afternoon of February 18 through the evening of February 19. A period of strong wind gusts accompanied the precipitation during the morning of January 19 and the snow level was around 7500 feet for much of the event. Much of the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall. The Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills generally picked up between an inch and 2 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELS picking up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall. Between a quarter and a half of an inch of rain fell across most of the Tehachapi Mountain range while the Kern County Deserts generally had less than a quarter inch of rainfall. Scattered thunderstorm broke out over the San Joaquin Valley  by late morning on January 19 and continued through late afternoon. Several of the  thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and small hail. There were also several reports of roadway flooding in the Visalia area. The precipitation tapered off by late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Tejon Hills measured a peak wind gust of 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system and an associated atmospheric river impacted Southern California on March 30th and 31st. This event saw impacts in the forms of heavy rain, some scattered thunderstorms, and snow to mountain communities above 5000 feet in elevation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds impacted the San Diego County Mountains on March 30th. Peak wind gusts were generally in the 35-50 mph range. Some notable higher gusts include 61 mph at Palomar Mountain Lookout, and 58 mph at the Harrison Park SDGE site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river sank south and stalled over Humboldt, Del Norte, and western Trinity counties, bringing widespread rains between 3 and 7 inches to the region. Rain fell over a short period of time, 8-10 hours, resulting in the highest flows on the Mad River and Redwood Creek since the 1970s. Widespread creek flooding occurred in Del Norte and Humboldt counties with multiple homes damages or destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on Highway 101 near Smith River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong Winds and Widespread Precipitation Impact Southern California on March 2. The strongest winds were reported in the mountains and along the desert slopes, where gusts over 70 mph occurred. Widely beneficial precipitation fell, but reports of flooding were received in the San Bernardino County Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred across the San Diego County Mountains from the early morning on March 2 through March 3. The strongest winds during the late morning through late evening on March 2. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm moved in from the south on February 4th, bringing heavy rain, strong winds, thunderstorms and heavy mountain snow through February 7th. Damaging winds brought down trees and caused widespread power outages. Flooding and strong winds resulted in multiple fatalities. Chain controls were observed in the mountains. Heavy rain brought nuisance flooding on roads and urban areas and rises to rivers, creeks and streams, with generally 1 to 3 inches of storm total rain reported across the Valley and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mather Field Airport reported a wind gust of 68 mph. Sacramento International Airport reported a wind gust of 64 mph. McClellan Airfield reported sustained winds of 43 mph with gusts to 59 mph, and a max gust of 66 mph. Vacaville Nut Tree Airport reported a gust of 59 mph. Numerous reports of downed power lines and trees across the area, closing roads and damaging cars. Sacramento County OES reported two fatalities from fallen trees, over 200,000 power outages, roof damages to airport facilities, multiple downed trees and road and park closures. The City of Sacramento estimated $350,000 total combined cost for storm damage. Yolo County OES reported over 4,000 power outages, school closures, and downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm moved in from the south on February 4th, bringing heavy rain, strong winds, thunderstorms and heavy mountain snow through February 7th. Damaging winds brought down trees and caused widespread power outages. Flooding and strong winds resulted in multiple fatalities. Chain controls were observed in the mountains. Heavy rain brought nuisance flooding on roads and urban areas and rises to rivers, creeks and streams, with generally 1 to 3 inches of storm total rain reported across the Valley and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Stockton Airport reported a max gust of 60 mph. Modesto Airport reported a max gust of 55 mph. California Highway Patrol reported multiple power lines down, downed trees, and road closures. Stanislaus County OES reported short-term power outages from the high winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported numerous downed trees and power lines due to high wind. A station in Placerville recorded a max gust of 64 mph. Nevada County Air Park reported a max gust of 52 mph. Calaveras Airport reported a max gust of 51 mph. Calaveras County OES reported scattered power outages throughout the county, removal of 55 downed trees from county roads, and 1 tree on a home. Tuolumne County OES reported multiple downed trees along roadways. Nevada County OES reported widespread power outages and downed trees across roads and into structures,  with moderate impacts to telecommunications. El Dorado County OES reported 40 calls for service related to trees and wires down, at least 5 buildings struck by trees, and 1 uninjured person trapped in a mobile home from a tree on top of the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of the winter storm which brought moderate to heavy precipitation and strong winds on February 4 and 5, a large upper trough moved into California during the morning of February 6 bringing with it another round of precipitation and gusty winds which continued until the morning of February 8. This system brought another half inch to an inch of rain to the San Joaquin Valley and 0.75 to 1.5 inches of liquid precipitation with 8 to 15 inchers of additional snowfall above 6000 feet in the Sierra Nevada. There were several reports of nuisance flooding as the ground was saturated by the previous storm at the onset of this storm. A fatal accident took place on SR 99 during the morning of February 6 as a result of the flooding. While winds were not as strong as with the previous system, several stations reported gusts exceeding 45 mph. The trough finally moved to the east of the area on February 8.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Cottonwood Creek West measured a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large slow moving upper low pressure system off the northern California Coast pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin which pushed into central California during the late afternoon of February 18 through the evening of February 19. A period of strong wind gusts accompanied the precipitation during the morning of January 19 and the snow level was around 7500 feet for much of the event. Much of the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall. The Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills generally picked up between an inch and 2 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELS picking up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall. Between a quarter and a half of an inch of rain fell across most of the Tehachapi Mountain range while the Kern County Deserts generally had less than a quarter inch of rainfall. Scattered thunderstorm broke out over the San Joaquin Valley  by late morning on January 19 and continued through late afternoon. Several of the  thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and small hail. There were also several reports of roadway flooding in the Visalia area. The precipitation tapered off by late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E station at Yosemite 49 measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system approached the central California coast on April 13 spreading increased clouds and precipitation into the interior of central California during the afternoon while temperatures plummeted as a cooler airmass pushed into the area. The most noticeable impact rom this system was strong wind gusts which accompanied the main cold front. Several reports of wind gusts exceeding 45 mph were reported during the early afternoon of April 13 while a few stations reported gusts above 60 mph. There were several law enforcement reports of trees downed in the San Joaquin Valley. This system also produced widespread precipitation across the area as the low moved across the area with several stations picking up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 5000 feet and several higher elevation SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 4 to 8 inches of new snowfall. The precipitation continued until the afternoon of April 14 when showers tapered off as the upper low moved to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported trees and power lines downed along south Union Ave.

CZ_NAME: WEST SIDE MOUNTAINS NORTH OF HIGHWAY 198
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough moved across California on April 4 and 5 bringing significant rainfall, heavy mountain snowfall and strong winds to the interior of central California. The precipitation began during the morning of April 4 from Fresno County northward. The precipitation then spread southward by the evening of April 4 and brought a cold and unstable airmass to the area which provided for showers and thunderstorms which produced small hail during the afternoon and evening of April 5. Several higher elevations SNOTELs picked up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall while precipitation totals were generally a quarter to half an inch of rain in the San Joaquin Valley, a half inch to an inch of liquid precipitation in the Sierra foothills and three quarters of an inch to an inch and a half of liquid precipitation in the higher Sierra Nevada. Snow levels were around 6000 feet at the onset of the precipitation during the morning of April 4, but rapidly lowered to around 3000 feet during the morning of April 5 as cooler air pushed into the area. Travel was adversely impacted in  the Sierra foothills and also in the Tehachapi Mountains where travel along Interstate 5 was halted by a one hour closure of the Tejon Pass during the evening of April 5 due to snow and strong winds. In addition to the precipitation several stations reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. The precipitation ended by the early morning of April 6 as the cold trough moved east into the Great Basin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Alcade RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough moved through central California on April 25 and 26. This system produced scattered showers across the area during the morning of April 26 and also brought a period of strong winds across the Mojave Desert slopes in Kern County between the afternoon of April 25 and the evening of April 26 where several stations reported gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several stations across the remainder of the Kern County mountains and deserts reported gusts exceeding 45 mph during this same time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Indian Wells Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 73 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system dropped southward off the northern California coast on March 29 which spread widespread precipitation into central California by evening. The low continued to drop further southward on March 30 which resulted in a period of moderate to heavy snowfall above 5000 feet in the Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains. Several higher elevation SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up between 10 and 20 inches of new snowfall. Much of the San Joaquin Valley, West Side Hills and Kern County Deserts picked up between a quarter and three quarters of an inch of rainfall while the Sierra foothills picked up between half an inch to an inch and half of rainfall. The higher elevations of the Sierra Nevada picked up between one and two inches of liquid precipitation. The low finally moved onshore over northern Baja on March 31 resulting in the precipitation slowly tapering off. Some thunderstorms did impact the San Joaquin Valley during the evening of March 31 producing gusty winds  and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a downed tree at the junction of SR 99 and SR 166.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved slowly eastward across the Pacific Northwest while an upper ridge remained off the California coast. Interaction between these two features produced a strong onshore wind gradient resulting in a period of gusty downslope winds along the Mojave Desert Slopes between the evening of April 29 and the evening of April 30. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system approached the central California coast on April 13 spreading increased clouds and precipitation into the interior of central California during the afternoon while temperatures plummeted as a cooler airmass pushed into the area. The most noticeable impact rom this system was strong wind gusts which accompanied the main cold front. Several reports of wind gusts exceeding 45 mph were reported during the early afternoon of April 13 while a few stations reported gusts above 60 mph. There were several law enforcement reports of trees downed in the San Joaquin Valley. This system also produced widespread precipitation across the area as the low moved across the area with several stations picking up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 5000 feet and several higher elevation SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 4 to 8 inches of new snowfall. The precipitation continued until the afternoon of April 14 when showers tapered off as the upper low moved to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a telephone pole down on Rd. 140 near Ave. 352.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low pressure moving through the Pacific Northwest brought heavy snow and multiple rounds of high wind to the eastern Sierra slopes. High wind also affected the Owens Valley and portions of the Mojave Desert.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two big rigs were blown over on Highway 395 near and 5 miles N of Cartago, and a sign was blown onto the highway near Olancha.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough moved across California on April 4 and 5 bringing significant rainfall, heavy mountain snowfall and strong winds to the interior of central California. The precipitation began during the morning of April 4 from Fresno County northward. The precipitation then spread southward by the evening of April 4 and brought a cold and unstable airmass to the area which provided for showers and thunderstorms which produced small hail during the afternoon and evening of April 5. Several higher elevations SNOTELs picked up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall while precipitation totals were generally a quarter to half an inch of rain in the San Joaquin Valley, a half inch to an inch of liquid precipitation in the Sierra foothills and three quarters of an inch to an inch and a half of liquid precipitation in the higher Sierra Nevada. Snow levels were around 6000 feet at the onset of the precipitation during the morning of April 4, but rapidly lowered to around 3000 feet during the morning of April 5 as cooler air pushed into the area. Travel was adversely impacted in  the Sierra foothills and also in the Tehachapi Mountains where travel along Interstate 5 was halted by a one hour closure of the Tejon Pass during the evening of April 5 due to snow and strong winds. In addition to the precipitation several stations reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. The precipitation ended by the early morning of April 6 as the cold trough moved east into the Great Basin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Indian Wells Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 72 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system moving southward along the Lower Colorado River Valley results in strong northerly winds across most of southeast CA during the morning and afternoon hours on the 14th, with the nearby observing sites reporting peak gusts in excess of 30-40 mph. The strong winds in particular damaged a mobile home at the NWS Cooperative Site at Chiraco Summit, CA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds resulted in damages to the mobile home at the NWS Cooperative Site located in Chiraco Summit. Damages included a metal awning and shingles stripped off. In addition, the exterior door to the utility room that faced the north-facing wall of the home was badly damaged. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low with an associated mature cyclone came to California on March 29, with widespread rain associated with the system. Continuing instability the next day resulted in noticeable thunderstorms with hail reported in several places across the Bay Area, including Walnut Creek, Belmont, and south of San Jose. In Monterey County, significant rainfall contributed to a landslide that forced the closure of California Highway 1 north of Big Sur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The PG&E operated station at Lime Creek Road recorded a wind gust of 62 miles per hour associated with a passing convective shower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low pressure system dropped slowly southward over Nevada and into southeast California on March 14. This low produced strong northeast winds over the Sierra Nevada and the Kern County Desert areas. Several stations measured winds gusts exceeding 45 mph with some stations picking up gusts in excess of 60 mph. The winds decreased in intensity by the evening of March 14 and dissipated by the afternoon of March 15.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of an 18 inch diameter tree being toppled an nearly hitting a house in Onyx. A nearby utility company weather station reported a wind gust of 62 mph near the time the tree fell.

In addition to the precipitation several stations reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. The precipitation ended by the early morning of April 6 as the cold trough moved east into the Great Basin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Cottonwood Canyon measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system approached the central California coast on April 13 spreading increased clouds and precipitation into the interior of central California during the afternoon while temperatures plummeted as a cooler airmass pushed into the area. The most noticeable impact rom this system was strong wind gusts which accompanied the main cold front. Several reports of wind gusts exceeding 45 mph were reported during the early afternoon of April 13 while a few stations reported gusts above 60 mph. There were several law enforcement reports of trees downed in the San Joaquin Valley. This system also produced widespread precipitation across the area as the low moved across the area with several stations picking up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 5000 feet and several higher elevation SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 4 to 8 inches of new snowfall. The precipitation continued until the afternoon of April 14 when showers tapered off as the upper low moved to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a downed tree on SR 65 1NW Porterville.

CZ_NAME: SAN BERNARDINO AND RIVERSIDE COUNTY VALLEYS - THE INLAND EMPIRE
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure entered the western US on March 13th. This allowed for a strong round of Santa Ana Winds to impact Southern California on March 13th and 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong Santa Ana winds impacted the Inland Empire on March 13th and 14th. Peak wind gusts were generally in the 45-60 mph range. Some notable higher gusts include 81 mph at Rancho Cucamonga (SCE1086), 77 mph at Verdemont (SCE603), and 65 mph at Chino Hills (SCE1729).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate stream north of mid-level monsoonal moisture coupled with instability and daytime heating enabled a 40-60% chance for thunderstorms during this brief period. Storms initiated over Mono County afternoon of the 2nd and worked their way north into northeast CA into the evening. There was a 40% chance of nocturnal thunderstorms across far western NV and northeast CA. Storms were projected to have the potential to produce localized flash flooding under stronger, training storm cores.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station XMLC1 (Mill Canyon) located 4.0 mi SSW of Walker, CA, reported  a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

CZ_NAME: MOUNTAINS OF SAN BENITO COUNTY AND INTERIOR MONTEREY COUNTY INCLUDING PINNACLES NATIONAL PARK
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly intensifying cyclone and atmospheric river combined from February 3rd to the 5th, creating a period of strong winds, heavy rain, and high surf. The entire region saw numerous impacts including roadway flooding, fallen trees, and power outages. Flood prone areas near rivers and along the coast were evacuated. Notable impacts also included pre-emptive closing of state parks, the cancelation of the San Francisco Half-Marathon, and the PGA canceling the final round of the Pebble Beach Pro-Am golf tournament. On Monday February 5, several schools were closed due to damage from the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lone Tree Towers station operated by PG&E recorded a wind gust of 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm moved in from the south on February 4th, bringing heavy rain, strong winds, thunderstorms and heavy mountain snow through February 7th. Damaging winds brought down trees and caused widespread power outages. Flooding and strong winds resulted in multiple fatalities. Chain controls were observed in the mountains. Heavy rain brought nuisance flooding on roads and urban areas and rises to rivers, creeks and streams, with generally 1 to 3 inches of storm total rain reported across the Valley and foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a downed tree on power lines on Meadow Ranch Road and Thatcher Mill Road with power lines lying across the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two storm systems impacted central California From February 17 to 20. A quickly-moving cold front came through the region on the 17th, causing minor nuisance flooding and gusty winds. A second, deeper but slower moving, system followed the next day with widespread rain and gusty winds. A warm front associated with this low came through the region late on Sunday the 18th, followed by the associated cold front and associated thunderstorms on Monday the 19th. With the longer period of rainfall and soils being saturated, shallow landslides were reported across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 3 foot diameter tree fell onto the roadway at California Route 236 and China Grade Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure system moved eastward across the Pacific Northwest and Northern California on May 4 and 5. This system brought widespread precipitation to our area between the early afternoon of May 4 and the morning of May 5 as moisture pushed southward through central California with much of the Sierra Nevada from Sierra National Park northward picking up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation with several higher elevation SNOTELs picking up an estimated 6 to 12 inches of new snowfall. Much of the San Joaquin Valley north of Kern County received between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall. While snow levels generally remained above 6000 feet for most of the event, elevations as low as 3000 feet received a dusting of snow during the morning of May 5. In addition to the precipitation, this system also brought unseasonably cool temperatures to the area on May 5 and 6 and also produced a period of strong wind gusts across the mountains and deserts in Kern County between the afternoon of May 4 and the early morning of May 6 with several stations measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Blue Max RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 73 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough moving through the Pacific Northwest on May 28 pushed a shortwave system through central California during the evening of May 28 and morning of May 29 producing an increased onshore pressure gradient. This in turn resulted in a period of strong wind gusts along the Mojave Desert slopes during the evening of May 28 and the early morning of May 29. Several stations reported gusts between 45 and 60 mph during this time frame. The winds decreased by late morning on May 29 as gradients relaxed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Southern California Edison station at Little Oak Canyon measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second of a pair of winter storms that was directed a bit more south of Lake Tahoe along the eastern Sierra into Mono county. This system brought additional snow amounts to much of the Sierra late Sunday into Monday with periods of lower elevation/valley rain, high elevation snow, and gusty winds. The main area of focus shifted from the Tahoe basin to Mono county where the White Mountains looked to get 2-3 inches of new snow while Mammoth Mountain was projected to receive another 5 to 10 inches of fresh snow. Snow levels hovered around 7000 feet for much of this second winter storm, then lowered to near 6000 feet towards the tail end of the storm late Monday. ECMWF EFI signals for CAPE across western Nevada showed higher instability with this second storm that indicated a better spillover potential and for showers to  persist well into Monday afternoon and evening. Storm liquid precipitation totals with this storm was projected to be about 1.50 inches to 2.00 inches along the span of the Sierra crest that translated to another 1 to 2 plus feet of snowfall for elevations above 8000 feet with this second storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station located at Palisades Tahoe summit, CA,  reported a peak wind gust of 97 mph.

CZ_NAME: SOUTH END OF THE UPPER SIERRA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure system moved eastward across the Pacific Northwest and Northern California on May 4 and 5. This system brought widespread precipitation to our area between the early afternoon of May 4 and the morning of May 5 as moisture pushed southward through central California with much of the Sierra Nevada from Sierra National Park northward picking up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation with several higher elevation SNOTELs picking up an estimated 6 to 12 inches of new snowfall. Much of the San Joaquin Valley north of Kern County received between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall. While snow levels generally remained above 6000 feet for most of the event, elevations as low as 3000 feet received a dusting of snow during the morning of May 5. In addition to the precipitation, this system also brought unseasonably cool temperatures to the area on May 5 and 6 and also produced a period of strong wind gusts across the mountains and deserts in Kern County between the afternoon of May 4 and the early morning of May 6 with several stations measuring gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bear Peak RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second, very strong, winter storm impacted Southwestern California. Rainfall totals were very significant across the area. Los Angeles county reported rain totals of 4 to 14 inches, Ventura county reported 2 to 10 inches, Santa Barbara county reported 4 to 12 inches and San Luis Obispo county reported 2 to 5 inches. With the heavy rainfall, numerous reports of flooding were received. In the mountains, very significant snowfall was reported with amounts in the 1 to 3 foot range. Also, severe thunderstorms and tornadoes were reported.

This storm���s warmer nature raised snow levels a bit higher(5500-6500 feet) with the potential for mixed precipitation in the valley areas and foothills. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active weather returns Wednesday afternoon to close out the month of January. Therefore, strong and gusty winds especially over higher mountain elevations was followed with mountain snow and valley rain showers. Wind was the first hazard expected starting over the Sierra and NE CA, then spreading into western Nevada with south winds between 30-50 mph, and gusts over 60-80 mph across higher mountain ridges and passes. Valley winds were projected to be 15-35 mph with gusts up to 55 mph. The probability of 60 mph winds across mountain areas in CA was 60% or greater and 10-20% over W NV. Lake winds were around 15-25 mph with gusts to 35-45 mph.||Valley rain and mountain snow showers were projected to move into the region   Wednesday PM. Global models and their respective ensemble members initially dug the trough and a deeper layer of sub-tropical IVT moisture will quickly sweep east and south across portions of northeast California, before descending into the Sierra, western Nevada, and Mono Co. Snow levels started near 7000 feet on Wednesday, then dropped to 6000 feet by Thursday. Most higher elevation areas received mostly rain initially before transitioning to mostly snow Thursday evening. Snow levels dropped to 5500 ft on Thursday afternoon, and dropped further to below 4000 feet by Friday morning. The Sierra above 7000 and Mono Co were given even chances for about 1-2 feet of new snow with 6-12 inches below 7000 feet. For northeast California 4-10 inches of accumulated snow, and up to 20 inches in the higher mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (LDRC1-Ladder Butte) located 17.0 mi S of Pittville, CA, and mesonet station Snag Hill Lookout (216PG) located 12.0 mi SSE of Bieber, CA, reported peak wind gusts of 65 mph and 59 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of storms pushed into the Sierra on Friday night, with periods of rain, high elevation snow, and gusty winds persisting into early the following week. High confidence followed for additional snow accumulations above 6500 feet, that included impacts to the Sierra passes overnight Friday into Saturday. Liquid water totals Friday PM through Sunday AM ranged from 0.20 to 0.75 inches along the eastern Sierra crest of Mono County to 0.60 to 2.00 inches farther north. Spillover precipitation east of the crest was limited with this storm, with 0.50 to 0.75 inches in the Tahoe Basin and a few hundredths to 0.50 inches along Highway 395. This equated to 8-16 inches of snowfall along the Sierra crest of Mono County to 8-18 inches along the crest farther north towards the Lake Tahoe basin. Although there was a lack of a significant wind signal during the period of both storms, ridge winds around the area still managed to approach 80+ mph. Otherwise, locally breezy conditions prevailed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station located at Palisades Tahoe summit, CA, reported a peak wind gust of 106 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large slow moving upper low pressure system off the northern California Coast pulled up a deep fetch of moisture of tropical origin which pushed into central California during the late afternoon of February 18 through the evening of February 19. A period of strong wind gusts accompanied the precipitation during the morning of January 19 and the snow level was around 7500 feet for much of the event. Much of the San Joaquin Valley and West Side Hills picked up between a half inch and an inch of rainfall. The Sierra Nevada and adjacent foothills generally picked up between an inch and 2 inches of liquid precipitation with several SNOTELS picking up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall. Between a quarter and a half of an inch of rain fell across most of the Tehachapi Mountain range while the Kern County Deserts generally had less than a quarter inch of rainfall. Scattered thunderstorm broke out over the San Joaquin Valley  by late morning on January 19 and continued through late afternoon. Several of the  thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall and small hail. There were also several reports of roadway flooding in the Visalia area. The precipitation tapered off by late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a large tree branch in the southbound lane of S. Clovis Ave. near the intersection of E. Butler Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Historic flooding occurred on January 22nd. Heavy rainfall occurred between 8 am and noon across western San Diego county. The heaviest rainfall occurred between 9 am and 11 am in the east side of the city of San Diego in Encanto and Lemon Grove to Spring Valley. Rainfall rates were observed at 1.5 to 2.5 inches in 60 minutes and resulted in 1000 year return intervals. Over 200 water rescues at homes, streets and in water channels were performed by San Diego Fire and Rescue. FEMA found 1000 homes damaged in the following weeks and 600 were declared major damage resulting in up to 31 million in damage claims and displaced residents, including 1200 households, in temporary shelters for several months. San Diego County EOC was activated on January 22 and remained activated at a low level into May 2024. The areas flooded the most were Mountain View, Encanto, Valencia Park, Shelltown, Southcrest, and Lincoln Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An eucalyptus tree was blown over and blocked all lanes on Sweetwater Road in Bonita. Wind gusts of 30-35 mph were recorded at the time. Severe trees were downed along I-805 and required emergency clean up by Caltrans. Strong winds from a thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of upper level tropical moisture pushed into central California from the southeast during the morning of July 13 and continued for much of the day. At first the thunderstorms were mainly dry as the lower and middle levels remained dry, and lightning from these thunderstorms ignited several new wildfires across the area. As the middle levels became more moist during the late afternoon and evening the thunderstorms became more in the way of rain producers. There were some locations in Yosemite National Park and the Upper San Joaquin River Basin which picked up between a quarter and a half inch of rainfall, but lower elevations generally only picked up a trace to a few hundredths of and inch of rain. There were also several reports of hail with one cell in Yosemite National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media report of a 20 year old hiker slipping and falling off of cables while descending Half Dome during a thunderstorm which was producing heavy rainfall and gusty winds. The fall resulted in fatal injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across Riverside County during the afternoon hours on the 24th. A severe thunderstorm developed near Desert Center, with strong downburst winds causing a semi-truck to flip on I-10. The overall environment was characterized by MLCAPE values of around 1000 J/KG as well as DCAPE values well in excess of 1500 J/KG, which were favorable parameters to get severe downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Semi-truck was flipped by strong wind gusts and was blocking most of the EB lanes on I-10 near Desert Center, causing traffic to back up. Winds gusts were estimated upwards of 55-60 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Historic flooding occurred on January 22nd. Heavy rainfall occurred between 8 am and noon across western San Diego county. The heaviest rainfall occurred between 9 am and 11 am in the east side of the city of San Diego in Encanto and Lemon Grove to Spring Valley. Rainfall rates were observed at 1.5 to 2.5 inches in 60 minutes and resulted in 1000 year return intervals. Over 200 water rescues at homes, streets and in water channels were performed by San Diego Fire and Rescue. FEMA found 1000 homes damaged in the following weeks and 600 were declared major damage resulting in up to 31 million in damage claims and displaced residents, including 1200 households, in temporary shelters for several months. San Diego County EOC was activated on January 22 and remained activated at a low level into May 2024. The areas flooded the most were Mountain View, Encanto, Valencia Park, Shelltown, Southcrest, and Lincoln Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were down on the road at SR-163 South and the Washington Street offramp.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remains of Atlantic Tropical Storm Alberto wrapped around high pressure centered over the Desert Southwest the pulled up north behind it into central California on June 24 and 25. The result of this was thunderstorms which had little to no precipitation with them on both days. One cluster of thunderstorms produced dry lightning which ignited several wildfires east of Reedley during the afternoon of June 24 which became known as the Fresno Lightning Complex Fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry lightning sparked several wildfires during the afternoon and evening of June 24 east of Reedley and Sanger. These fires became known as the Fresno Lightning Complex which resulted in several evacuations as the fires spread rapidly due to heavy fuel loading. The transmitter for KVPT Channel 18 was destroyed by one of the fires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture from the remnants of Atlantic Tropical Storm Alberto surged north up the Gulf of California and into the Mojave Desert, fueling isolated thunderstorms. One storm produced high winds.
Gusty northerly winds behind a late-season cold front funneled down the Owens Valley, causing isolated damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew a tree into the southbound lanes of Sunland Drive 1 mile WSW of Bishop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep trough brought widespread hail showers and snow to Northwest California. Heavy snow of several feet was measured in the mountains while the hail showers resulted in numerous traffic accidents along the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforement reported Non-Thunderstorm associated wind damamge 1 NNE Fort Bragg, CA. Lat. 39.45N, Long. -123.80W.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level wave caused westerly/onshore winds to increase, becoming strong and gusty over the mountains and deserts of Southern California. Many of the typical wind-prone canyons and passes saw very high wind gusts occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly winds impacted the San Gorgonio Pass starting early in the afternoon on April 24th and lasted through the afternoon on April 26th. Notable peak wind gusts include: 75 mph at the Whitewater RAWS site, 60-65 mph at other sites near Whitewater, and 58 mph at Desert Hot Springs (SCE566).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly winds impacted the Apple and Lucerne Valleys starting early in the afternoon on April 24th and lasted through the afternoon on April 26th. Notable peak wind gusts include 58 mph at Lucerne Valley (SCE262) and 56 mph at the Victorville Airport (KVCV).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low pressure system approached the central California coast on April 13 spreading increased clouds and precipitation into the interior of central California during the afternoon while temperatures plummeted as a cooler airmass pushed into the area. The most noticeable impact rom this system was strong wind gusts which accompanied the main cold front. Several reports of wind gusts exceeding 45 mph were reported during the early afternoon of April 13 while a few stations reported gusts above 60 mph. There were several law enforcement reports of trees downed in the San Joaquin Valley. This system also produced widespread precipitation across the area as the low moved across the area with several stations picking up between a half inch and an inch of liquid precipitation. The precipitation mainly fell as snow above 5000 feet and several higher elevation SNOTELs in the Sierra Nevada picked up an estimated 4 to 8 inches of new snowfall. The precipitation continued until the afternoon of April 14 when showers tapered off as the upper low moved to the east of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: California Highway Patrol reported a downed tree blocking the roadway 1S Oildale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough moved through central California on April 25 and 26. This system produced scattered showers across the area during the morning of April 26 and also brought a period of strong winds across the Mojave Desert slopes in Kern County between the afternoon of April 25 and the evening of April 26 where several stations reported gusts exceeding 60 mph. Several stations across the remainder of the Kern County mountains and deserts reported gusts exceeding 45 mph during this same time period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jawbone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cold upper trough moved across California on April 4 and 5 bringing significant rainfall, heavy mountain snowfall and strong winds to the interior of central California. The precipitation began during the morning of April 4 from Fresno County northward. The precipitation then spread southward by the evening of April 4 and brought a cold and unstable airmass to the area which provided for showers and thunderstorms which produced small hail during the afternoon and evening of April 5. Several higher elevations SNOTELs picked up an estimated 12 to 20 inches of new snowfall while precipitation totals were generally a quarter to half an inch of rain in the San Joaquin Valley, a half inch to an inch of liquid precipitation in the Sierra foothills and three quarters of an inch to an inch and a half of liquid precipitation in the higher Sierra Nevada. Snow levels were around 6000 feet at the onset of the precipitation during the morning of April 4, but rapidly lowered to around 3000 feet during the morning of April 5 as cooler air pushed into the area. Travel was adversely impacted in  the Sierra foothills and also in the Tehachapi Mountains where travel along Interstate 5 was halted by a one hour closure of the Tejon Pass during the evening of April 5 due to snow and strong winds. In addition to the precipitation several stations reported wind gusts exceeding 60 mph. The precipitation ended by the early morning of April 6 as the cold trough moved east into the Great Basin area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Laural Mountain RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level low pressure system moving across the Desert Southwest resulted in gusty westerly winds across portions of southeast CA. Imperial County Airport ASOS nearby recorded peak wind gusts ranging between 35 and 40 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree blown onto road leading to a road closure of Bennett Road from Even Hewes Highway to Ross Road. Imperial County Airport nearby reported peak wind gusts up to 35-40 mph. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms impacted the Bay Area on April 4 and 5 as a cold core upper level low tracked over the region. After a front passed through the region, an unstable atmosphere set up over the region and contributed to convective activity. The SPC placed the region in a general mention of thunderstorms, with small hail being the main impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large pine tree was blown down near Soquel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A second, late season, storm rolled across Southwestern California. This storm brought strong north to northeast winds as well as strong thunderstorms with gusty outflow winds  and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northeast winds were observed across the Santa Susana Mountains. Some peak gusts include: Browns Canyon (gust 78 MPH), Palo Sola Truck Road (gust 72 MPH, Wiley Ridge RAWS (gust 66 MPH) and South Mountain Lookout (gust 60 MPH).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Saturday September 7, a monsoonal easterly wind flow weather pattern set up and thunderstorms developed across the mountains and spread across the Inland Empire. Some of these storms had very heavy rainfall and prolific lightning. The actively burning Line fire was affected and helped develop additional persistent thunderstorms. On Sunday September 8, thunderstorms were most active in the Inland Empire where flash flooding and strong winds occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts from thunderstorms measured at 50 mph along Nuevo Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a prolonged heat wave with near record highs across much of the area between September 4 and September 9, a trough moved through the region on September 10 and 11 bringing much cooler temperatures to the area along with a period of increased winds during the evening of September 11. The winds were most noticeable over eastern Kern County where several stations measured gusts exceeding 45 mph and some low impact indicator sites had some gusts exceeding 60 mph. The winds decreased during the morning of September 12 as the trough moved to the east of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jawbone Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough that reached the west coast on the 10th brought dropped an attendant cold front southeast into western Nevada that increased west to southwest winds over the regional. Guidance projected winds to mix down into most valley areas with gusts peaking at 30-35 mph with a good chance for some wind prone areas seeing 50 mph. Higher elevation wind gusts peaked early in the day with peak ridge level wind gusts of 50-60 mph. This resulted in impacts for recreation, air/road travel, and fire weather concerns.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH03) located 1.7 mi SE of Mammoth Mountain Ski Summit reported a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Guidance showed an upper level low opening into a trough and passing over northern California into western Nevada during the evening hours the 2nd. The attendant cold front produced gusty southwest winds throughout the day into the evening. Ridgetop wind gusts were projected to increased to around 50-70 mph by the morning of the 2nd, and last through the day and overnight. These winds allowed for critical fire weather conditions, choppy waters on area lakes, aviation impacts, and blowing dust near desert areas. Higher resolution model guidance also suggested the potential for showers and thunderstorms along and east of US-95 during the afternoon hours of the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH02) located 1.5 mi SE of Mammoth Mountain reported a peak wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough moved into central California on October 16 bringing some light precipitation to the area between the afternoon of October 16 and the afternoon of October 17 along with a period of increased winds between the evening of October 16 and the morning of October 18. Precipitation amounts were fairly paltry with most of the area picking up trace amounts to a few hundredths of an inch although portions of Yosemite National Park picked up between a quarter and a half inch of liquid precipitation with the snow level running around 9000 feet. This system also produced strong wind gusts along the Sierra Nevada and Tehachapi Mountains as well as across the Mojave Desert Slopes in eastern Kern County. Several stations measured gusts in excess of 45 mph while a few low impact indicator stations measured gusts in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mount Tom RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm brought more impactful weather to the region starting on Sunday, the 27th, that continued into the beginning of the work week. Forecast guidance showed a Pacific Northwest trough making its way across western CONUS with the trough axis passing over the CWA on late Monday afternoon. This pattern aloft allowed a quick moving cold front to bring gusty southwest winds during the day on Sunday, 26th October, and wintry precipitation chances on that continued into through the early part of the week. The latest NBM probabilities for wind gusts of 40 mph or greater on the 26th showed the Lake Tahoe Basin having around a 40-60% chance of occurrence with the Reno area seeing around a 30-40% chance. A Lake Wind Advisory was therefore issued for Lake Tahoe and Pyramid Lake for Sunday afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (MTH02) located 1.5 mi SE of Mammoth Mountain, a mesonet station (XMLC1-Mill Canyon) located 4.0 mi SSW of Walker, CA, and a mesonet station (565SE Sce-3577) located  4.0 mi NNE of Twin Lakes, CA, reported a peak wind gust of 72 mph, 62 mph, and 60 mph respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low-pressure system moved into the western United States, it resulted in a tightening of the pressure gradient. As a result, strong westerly winds materialized across western portions of Imperial County, where isolated gusts in excess of 55 mph were observed during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph wind gust was measured at the Imperial County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This stronger of a one-two shot of storm systems advanced an east and south moving cold front inside slider-like mainly near and north of US-50. The bands of showers, and embedded thunder, also included very small hail as well. This front brought gusty northerly winds (30-40+ mph), choppy conditions especially for Pyramid Lake, and sharply colder temperatures. Shower and storm activity peaked during the mid-late afternoon, and then winded down as it moved south of US-50 by early this evening. Snow levels also dropped quickly to near lake level around the Lake Tahoe Basin early evening with a few snow showers remaining in the mix as well. Farther south into Mono County, a short period of upslope flow behind the front lead to minor snow accumulations near and west of US-395. The combination of the amplified northerly flow aloft, and persistent cold air advection allowed a few sites to receive up to 3 of additional lake-effect snowfall enhancement on larger lakes like Mono Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station located at the Palisades Tahoe at Alpine Meadows ski resort reported a peak wind gust of 101 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system passing to the north of region, tightened pressure and thermal gradient across the region that resulted in wind gusts up to 45 mph with higher gusts across higher terrain, especially up along the Oregon border. This created impacts to recreation, fire weather, and aviation. These conditions required issuance of a Red Flag Warning for NE-CA and NW-NV Friday, as well as issuance of Lake Wind Advisories for Lake Tahoe and Pyramid Lake late morning through early evening Friday. Increased turbulence early Friday morning resulted in bumpy commercial flights locally to include potential for areas of blowing dust, and low visibilities, along Hwy 50, Hwy 95, and I-80 out east in the Basin and Range. This included a slight (10-15%) chance of rain showers Friday in Mono and Mineral counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station (HVRTM) Blue Streak Weather station located 1.0 mi NNW of the Heavenly Ski Summit, CA reported a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough slowly moved eastward across California on August 24 and brought gusty winds to portions of the Sierra Nevada and Kern County Desert areas. Several stations recorded gusts exceeding 45 mph during the afternoon and evening and August 24 with a few low impact indicator sites measuring gusts exceeding 55 mph. This system also brought cooler than normal temperatures to the area for a few days as well as some light precipitation. The trough moved east of the area during the morning of August 25 and winds decreased as the trough moved out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Indian Wells Canyon RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 64 mph.
